item.title: I like this a lot
item.content:encoded:
It's wonderfully bleak, and the whole article comes together to really reinforce that feeling. I like that there's no caption on the picture, I like that there's no list of previous instances or experiment logs, and most of all I like that this is 231 words long and no longer. This article perfectly captures a feeling that very things I've ever read have managed, and a big part of that is accomplished by keeping it so barebones.

10/10 from me, and probably one of my top 50 scips.

item.title: how to disappear completely
item.content:encoded:
probably my favorite CWW article, though I'd have to think on it.

In pretty much every respect, it's a mirror to the reader. Is this article sad? Scary? Bittersweet? Those are questions for you to answer.

I will restate a suggestion given some time ago that a small collapsible listing the previous vehicles affected by the anomaly would be nice. It would not "add" much to the piece, strictly speaking, but I prefer it when articles still maintain a feeling of "SCP-ness" even if they're things like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somebody dead,nobody know.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, this is probably one of my favorite articles on the wiki for how well it portrays its mood in such a short space. Also, Nightcall is always a win.

https://youtu.be/s77MlzOF67k

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know I really like the idea of this, like this just give you a chance to start over again and leave every thing behind.

I suggest listening to Spaceman by The Killers while listening to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a REALLY good example of very unsettling, very understated horror. This is an excellent example of what a horror SCP should look like, of what a short SCP should look like. Good job, Scan.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: …that's almost 40 revisions to an article that isn't theirs. Yikes. Thanks for bringing this to staff attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that I've reverted changes made by a user named "JayPlaysBeamNG", as they added multiple addenda to the article, and it appears that they didn't get permission from CWW. If this was in error, please revert my revert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, guess I miscounted. Still, I don't really feel like going back to change anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, according to the short works hub and wordcounter, 173 is 236 words when including the caption, and this is 231 words. So "An instance of SCP-3707" would still keep you within that margin by exactly one word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted this article to be no longer than 173, so if I'd added a caption I would've had to take out a word or two somewhere else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The lack of a caption for the picture is weirdly unsettling. I honestly don't know why you wouldn't just have the caption be "An instance of SCP-3707".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised they haven't tried putting a camera in there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a great Series I article. I love the sense of horror given in so few words. It's really just a great piece of work. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the skip, it's ability to give a sense of isolation and loneliness to the reader through the lack of friends and families concern is frightening.
My only issue, and the part that kinda breaks it for me is that it's stated the driver feels compulsion to drive away from their home and keep driving with no particular destination. They disappear and the vehicle is found far away. And there it is. How is it that an abandoned vehicle came to be owned by the driver. Abandoned vehicles will in most cases be eventually towed and held by the city for some time. At the end of the article it says, in so many words, the vehicle isn't content just sitting there. It will disappear from time to time. If this is the case, how would it eventually be auctioned if it won't stay put…
*Sorry about any grammar issues. It's not a superpower I seem to possess…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great example of how a short form skip should work. It's short, sweet, and satisfying.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fairly close to home… the end goal may not be something that I want, but the random night driving is definitely something that I do. I also partially canon that the article and procedures are so brief because the Foundation aren't immune to the effects, so it's a bit like SCP-___-J.

805 kilometers seems like a lot though. Edit: I guess that's only about 8 hours of highway driving, so that's not too bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took a picture and added it to the article. Maybe I'll see if something taken around sunset wouldn't be better, idk. I'm going out tomorrow anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Short and sweet, with monkeys on its feet.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This.

One of the few SCPs I've read where I don't actually want anymore information. It's succinctness makes it all the more melancholy. Brilliantly done.
+1

item.title: Re: If you could leave, would you?
item.content:encoded:
And yet this article has 40+ upvotes.

Oh, and another upvote from me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great.

In my headcanon, there's a reason the Foundation keeps it maintained: it's offered as a retirement option for burnt-out senior employees who are, quote, "getting too old for this shit".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of some of the Series 1 SCPs in a good way. Very minimal, gives exactly what you need to know and nothing more. If there was a hall of fame for short SCPs, this should be in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing much anyone else hasn't said. The thing that's really great here is the length; you establish an impactful, tangible theme that sticks with the reader past - whenever. It has duration, which is kind of amazing given the space in which it's conveyed. There's like entire volumes on the site of documentation and crap that failed to make me feel as much as you did here. I'm a little disappointed you didn't follow through with what seemed like an obvious test log - watch the car for as long as possible - but considering the final product it's probably better you didn't. The melancholy imparted by the sparseness of it is depth enough to make it satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple. Short. Plays on the fundamental human fear of being completely replaceable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest while i do think that was a bit overthinking, i love the idea that this is a "anti-memetic suicide".

It's so off-putting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How you react to the thought of totally disappearing, never to be missed, probably depends a lot on how you feel about your life and the people in it.

Cool song. Actually, the vibe I was going for with this article is the same one I got from Nightcall cuz I listened to it on repeat while writing. So that's, like, the "canonical" answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's… not that deep. What you see is what you get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing this is about some sort of antimemetic suicide or alien abduction or something? Because I don't get it personally >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alternative theme? (though Nightcall is an excellent choice)

I'm weirdly not even sure if this is sad or happy. It could go either way.

Anyway, the bit about it changing 4 times in the past is what put it over the top for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is terrific. Appropriate coming from the guy who popularized the rule of thumb. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Would that really add anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sad story, I enjoyed. However, I have one suggestion:

Perhaps, considering the car changes every now and then, you have a collapsible or a footnote listing previous vehicles it has been?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, what with 4000 SCPs out there, I'd feel honored to get in someone's top 100. Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addressed both — changed 'new' to 'different', which should at least imply more strongly that it's not necessarily a 2002 Camry, and briefly described the tested individuals.

I'm trying to keep this to 233 words max, since this is practice for a hypothetical future short works contest. Found some chaff to cut down for this, can't guarantee that that'll be the case for all recommended changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP feels… quiet. In a good way. +1

I'd like to suggest briefly describing "tested individuals" (ie, such as coworkers or relatives or somesuch).

So the car they had vanishes entirely, but an entirely different 2002 Camry with Minnesota plates will appear with the same properties?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the feeling of subtle isolation this brings. It definitely implies that the people driving these cars (at least originally) are those who never connected greatly with others, who can simply disappear. What a sad feeling, though one I've overwhelmingly felt before.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've stated before that what I look for most in a skip is humanity and brevity. What you've done here doesn't hit my top ten, and it certainly doesn't blow me away: but it does give me a very cold and distant feeling that I'm finding it hard to express. I like it.

+1. You made me feel something different.

item.title: If you could leave, would you?
item.content:encoded:
Coldpost written in the car.

Mood music: Nightcall, by Kavinsky

Image is my own original work.
